title: Youth and Student Activism
subtitle: How Young People Moved the Civil Rights Movement
overview: Across the Civil Rights History Project archive, young people are on record taking action for fair treatment. Students at Adkin High School in Kinston, North Carolina walked out of class in 1951 and drew up a list of what their school needed; an eight-year-old in Oklahoma City made a motion to sit at a drug-store counter until served; college students joined sit-ins and went to jail. This unit grounds each grade band in those first-person accounts so learners hear young people describe their own decisions in their own words.

bands.label: Early Elementary
bands.skill_focus: Listen to a first-person story from a recording and name what a person did and how they might have felt.
bands.concept: Young people, even children, can ask for fair treatment and act together to get it.
objectives: Listen to a short oral-history clip and retell one thing the young people did.
Name a feeling a person in the clip might have had.
Identify what the students or children asked for.
Give one example of children working together.

materials.clip_title: Is There a School in Our Area Like This?
materials.why: A class discussion in a weekly reader asks what an ideal school looks like, an entry point a young child can picture.

materials.clip_title: Just Sit Until They Served Us
materials.why: An eight-year-old makes a motion to sit until served, a leader the same age as the listeners.

materials.name: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
materials.why: Her page lets the class meet the child from the sit-in clip and hear her voice.

materials.name: John Dudley et al
materials.why: The page anchors the Adkin High School story to the students who told it.

activities: Play the Adkin clip [e64 4001-4165s]. Ask the class to picture a school where everything is fair, then draw one thing a fair school would have.
Play the Oklahoma City clip [e82 382-512s]. Tell the class the girl who made the plan was eight years old. Have learners show with their bodies what sitting and waiting looks like.
As a group, make a class chart titled What the Children Asked For, with the teacher writing learner answers.
Sing or read aloud the words the children said, then have learners repeat one short phrase from the clip together.
discussion_questions: What did the students in the first story want for their school?
How old was the girl who made the plan to sit down? Is that close to your age?
How might the children have felt while they waited?
When have you worked together with friends to fix something that was not fair?
bands.assessment: Each learner draws a picture of the young people taking action and tells the teacher one sentence about what they did. The teacher writes the sentence under the picture.
bands.content_note: Both clips at this band stay on the fair-school request and the sit-down plan. The harsher treatment the children met at the counter is not played for K-2; route around it and keep to the deciding-and-acting part of the story.
standards: C3 D2.Civ.2.K-2: Explain how all people, not just official leaders, play important roles in a community.
NCSS Theme 10 (Civic Ideals and Practices), early grades: people can act together for the common good.

bands.label: Upper Elementary
bands.skill_focus: Sequence the steps a group took and explain why each step mattered.
bands.concept: Young people organized: they named a problem, made a plan, and acted together to ask for change.
objectives: Put the steps of the Adkin walkout in order from the clips.
Explain why the students kept their teacher out of their plan.
Compare two ways young people asked for fair treatment.
Describe what a petition is and why the students wrote one.

materials.clip_title: Is There a School in Our Area Like This?
materials.why: Shows where the walkout started, in a Wednesday civics class reading a weekly reader.
Shows the eight-item list, the choice to protect their teacher, and the meeting they won permission to hold.
An NAACP trip north where Oklahoma City children used the same water fountain as everyone for the first time, the experience behind the sit-in plan.

materials.clip_title: Just Sit Until They Served Us
materials.why: The Youth Council meeting where the plan to sit until served was made.

materials.name: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
materials.why: Links the sit-in clips to the person and the wider archive.

materials.name: John Dudley et al
materials.why: Links the walkout clips to the students who described them.

activities: Play both Adkin clips [e64 4001-4165s, then 4165-4317s]. As a class, build a numbered timeline: civics class question, students draw up eight items, teacher kept out, permission won to meet in the auditorium.
Define petition together. In small groups, learners write three items they would put on a list for an ideal school, modeled on the students' eight items.
Play the two Oklahoma City clips [e82 242-382s, then 382-512s]. Discuss how the trip north connected to the decision to sit at Katz Drug Store.
Have learners write two or three sentences answering: why did the students keep their teacher out of the plan?
discussion_questions: What happened first in the Adkin story, and what happened next?
Why did the students decide not to involve their teacher?
How did the trip north change what the Oklahoma City children expected?
Walking out of class and sitting at a counter are two different actions. What did each one ask for?
bands.assessment: Each learner produces a labeled four-step timeline of the Adkin walkout drawn from the clips, plus one sentence explaining why the students protected their teacher.
bands.content_note: Clips chosen here stay on planning and action. The treatment the sit-in children met is acknowledged in plain language only if a learner raises it; it is not the focus and no graphic detail is required at this band.
standards: C3 D2.Civ.6.3-5: Describe ways in which people benefit from and are challenged by working together.
C3 D2.His.1.3-5: Create and use a chronological sequence of related events to compare developments that happened at the same time.
NCSS Theme 6 (Power, Authority, and Governance), middle grades.

bands.label: Middle School
bands.skill_focus: Analyze a person's decision to act, weighing motive, risk, and who should lead.
bands.concept: Choosing to act carried personal risk, and movements made deliberate choices about who would lead a given challenge.
objectives: Explain what moved a young person to act on conscience using evidence from a clip.
Identify the risks named or implied in each account.
Analyze the reasoning behind a decision about who should lead a sit-in.
Cite a specific clip when making a claim about a person's choice.

materials.why: A student-run process: eight items, a protected teacher, a self-organized meeting won from the principal.

materials.clip_title: Just Sit Until They Served Us
materials.why: A child's motion at a Youth Council meeting becomes a coordinated action.

materials.clip_title: Hypocrites as Soon as We Left Church
materials.why: A young person measures a segregated church against the song that Jesus loves all children, the conscience root of a later decision.
A college freshman joins the Durham sit-ins, pickets and sits at lunch counters, and goes to jail twice: action carrying clear risk.

materials.clip_title: I'm a White Boy and I Don't Need to Be Leading
materials.why: Organizers conclude a Black student should lead the challenge to segregated lunch counters: a direct case for analyzing who leads and why.

materials.name: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
materials.why: Connects the conscience-and-action clips to her full account and to the wider sit-in record in the archive.

materials.name: James Oscar Jones
materials.why: Anchors the who-leads clip to the organizer who described the decision.

activities: Play the two Joan Trumpauer Mulholland clips [e60 184-399s, then 954-1122s]. Learners chart the move from a childhood observation about a segregated church to a freshman-year decision to sit in and risk jail.

Play the James Oscar Jones clip [e57 2242-2520s]. Hold a structured discussion: the organizers decided a Black student should lead the lunch-counter challenge. Have learners list the reasons such a decision might be made and evaluate them.
In pairs, learners build a Decision and Risk two-column note for one young person across the clips: what they decided, and what they risked.
Each learner writes a short claim-evidence paragraph: name one young person's choice, then cite the clip (entry and time) that supports the claim.
discussion_questions: What did Joan Trumpauer Mulholland notice as a child, and how is it connected to what she did in college?
What risks did joining a sit-in carry for a young person?
Why might organizers decide that a Black student should lead the lunch-counter challenge?
Compare a child's motion in Oklahoma City with a college freshman's choice in Durham. What is similar about how the decision was made?
bands.assessment: A one-paragraph claim-evidence-reasoning response: state what moved one young person to act, cite the specific clip (entry number and timestamp), and explain the reasoning, plus a short note evaluating the who-should-lead decision in the James Oscar Jones clip.
bands.content_note: Jail is named in the Joan Trumpauer Mulholland account and should be discussed plainly as a consequence young people accepted. No physical-violence clip is assigned at this band; if learners ask about violence, acknowledge it occurred and route detailed treatment to the 9-12 band.
standards: C3 D2.Civ.7.6-8: Apply civic virtues and democratic principles in school and community settings.
C3 D2.His.4.6-8: Analyze multiple factors that influenced the perspectives of people during different historical eras.
NCSS Theme 5 (Individuals, Groups, and Institutions), middle grades.

bands.label: High School
bands.skill_focus: Evaluate primary-source testimony, critique how history is represented, and analyze structural barriers.
bands.concept: Young people confronted not only individual acts of exclusion but the structures that produced them, including how history is taught and who is allowed to take an exam.
objectives: Evaluate an oral-history account as a primary source, distinguishing testimony from interpretation.
Critique how a textbook image represented Reconstruction and analyze its effect on a student in the classroom.
Analyze a structural barrier and distinguish it from an individual act of exclusion.
Construct an evidence-based argument about how young people challenged structures, citing specific clips.

materials.why: A college student's sit-in participation and two jailings, read as primary-source testimony of direct action.

materials.clip_title: I'm a White Boy and I Don't Need to Be Leading
materials.why: Evidence for analyzing leadership and positionality within an organizing decision.

materials.clip_title: The Same Illustration in My Textbook
materials.why: The only image of Black Reconstruction in her AP history textbook was drawn from Birth of a Nation, and white classmates glanced at her: a primary case for textbook critique and how history is told.
About a dozen Black students from South Carolina State and Claflin were barred from taking the Graduate Record Exam at the University of South Carolina: a clear structural barrier to analyze.

materials.name: Judy Richardson
materials.why: Connects the textbook-critique clip to her full oral history and to her later documentary work in the archive.

materials.name: Thomas Walter Gaither
materials.why: Anchors the GRE-barrier clip to the organizer's account.

materials.name: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
materials.why: Provides the direct-action testimony alongside the structural-barrier sources.

activities: Play the Judy Richardson clip [e69 1564-1788s]. Learners analyze the source of the textbook image (Birth of a Nation) and write a short critique of how a textbook can shape what a classroom believes about Reconstruction, including the effect on the one Black student present.

Play the Thomas Walter Gaither clip [e119 1692-1872s]. In a structured analysis, learners distinguish a structural barrier (being barred from sitting the GRE) from an individual act of exclusion, and identify who held the power in each.

Play the Joan Trumpauer Mulholland and James Oscar Jones clips [e60 954-1122s, e57 2242-2520s]. Learners treat each as a primary source, noting what is testimony and what is the speaker's later interpretation.

Learners construct an evidence-based argument (essay or seminar position) answering how young people challenged structures and not only individuals, citing at least three clips by entry number and timestamp.
discussion_questions: How does an oral-history account differ as evidence from a textbook account of the same period?
What did it do to a classroom that the only image of Black Reconstruction came from Birth of a Nation? What did it do to the one Black student in the room?
How is being barred from taking the GRE different from being refused service at one lunch counter? What changes when the barrier is structural?
Across these accounts, where did young people aim at a structure rather than at a single person, and how can you tell?
bands.assessment: An evidence-based argument (essay, document-based response, or graded seminar) on how young people challenged structures as well as individual exclusion, citing at least three clips by entry number and timestamp and distinguishing primary-source testimony from interpretation. A source-critique of the textbook image in the Judy Richardson clip is required as one body section.
bands.content_note: This band names exclusion, jail, the Birth of a Nation source of the textbook image, and a barred examination directly, as the historical record states them. Frame the Birth of a Nation reference as the documented source of a textbook illustration, not as material to screen. No assigned clip contains graphic violence; if learners pursue violence in the wider movement, direct them to vetted archive material with the audit-tier and citation context intact.
standards: C3 D2.His.6.9-12: Analyze the ways in which the perspectives of those writing history shaped the history that they produced.
C3 D2.His.10.9-12: Detect possible limitations in various kinds of historical evidence and differing secondary interpretations.
C3 D2.Civ.14.9-12: Analyze historical, contemporary, and emerging means of changing societies and promoting the common good.
NCSS Theme 2 (Time, Continuity, and Change), high school.

0.reading_level: Kindergarten read-aloud; teacher reads every word, learners respond orally.
0.essential_question: What did the kids do to make things fair?

vocabulary.definition: When everyone gets the same chance.
The place where you go to learn.
To say you want something.

0.scaffolding: Picture cards for each vocabulary word; learners point and repeat; answers given by drawing or speaking, not writing.

1.reading_level: Grade 1 read-aloud with simple shared-reading phrases.
1.essential_question: How did young people ask for a fair school?

vocabulary.definition: Everyone is treated the same way.
A paper where people write what they want and sign their names.
Doing something as a group, not alone.

1.scaffolding: Sentence frame: The students asked for ____. Learners fill the blank aloud or with a drawing; vocabulary shown with a picture and one short sentence.

2.reading_level: Grade 2; learners read short captions and the teacher reads the clip framing.
2.essential_question: What problem did the young people see, and what did they do about it?

vocabulary.definition: Everyone is treated the same and given the same chance.
A written list of what a group asks for, signed by the people who agree.
When people sit down in a place and stay until they are treated fairly.

2.scaffolding: Two-step sentence frame: The young people saw ____. So they ____. Learners complete it in writing with a word bank.

3.reading_level: Grade 3; learners read short clip-derived summaries independently.
3.essential_question: What steps did young people take to ask for change?

vocabulary.definition: A written request signed by many people who want the same thing.
When students leave class on purpose to show a problem needs fixing.
When people refuse to use a place or buy something until it changes.

3.scaffolding: Numbered timeline organizer with the first step filled in; learners add the next three steps from the clips.

4.reading_level: Grade 4; multi-sentence summaries and short quotations.
4.essential_question: How did young people plan and carry out their actions for change?

vocabulary.definition: A formal written request, signed by supporters, that asks people in charge to make a change.
Laws or rules that kept Black people and white people apart in public places.
To plan and arrange people so they can act together toward a goal.

4.scaffolding: Cause-and-effect organizer (the children went on a trip north, so they expected ____); word bank with the three vocabulary terms.

5.reading_level: Grade 5; paragraph-length sources and guided close reading.
5.essential_question: Why did young people choose the methods they used, and how did one action connect to the next?

vocabulary.definition: A formal written document presenting a group's demands or requests to an authority, validated by the signatures of its supporters.
The legally enforced separation of people by race in schools, transportation, and other public facilities.
A planned action, such as a sit-in or a walkout, that seeks change without using physical force.

5.scaffolding: Close-reading guide with margin questions; learners annotate a clip summary and link two events with a because statement.

6.reading_level: Grade 6; analytical reading of clip summaries and short quotations.
6.essential_question: What moved young people to act, even when it was risky?

vocabulary.definition: An inner sense of what is right and wrong that can guide a person to act.
Refusing to obey a law on purpose, peacefully, because you believe the law is unjust.
An NAACP group that organized young people for civil rights activity.

6.scaffolding: Decision-and-risk two-column note with one row completed as a model; sentence starter: She decided to ____ even though ____.

7.reading_level: Grade 7; claim-and-evidence reading with citation practice.
7.essential_question: How did personal conscience lead young people to take public action?

vocabulary.definition: A person's moral judgment about right and wrong that can prompt action despite cost.
The deliberate, peaceful refusal to comply with a law regarded as unjust, accepting the legal consequences.
How a person's identity, such as their race, shapes their role and standing in a situation.

7.scaffolding: Claim-evidence frame: I claim ____ because in clip ____ the speaker says ____; learners must attach an entry number and timestamp.

8.reading_level: Grade 8; multi-source analysis and short argumentative writing.
8.essential_question: How did young people weigh conscience, risk, and the question of who should lead?

vocabulary.definition: The internal moral compass that can move a person to act against an injustice even at personal risk.
A principled, nonviolent breach of an unjust law undertaken openly, with willingness to bear the penalty as part of the protest.
The way a person's social location, including race and status, affects their authority to speak or lead in a given context.

8.scaffolding: Structured evaluation grid for the who-should-lead decision (reason, strength, counterpoint); learners then write a claim-evidence-reasoning paragraph.

9.reading_level: Grade 9; primary-source analysis with guided source-criticism.
9.essential_question: What can a young person's own testimony tell us that a textbook cannot?

vocabulary.definition: A firsthand record, such as an oral-history interview, made by someone who took part in or witnessed an event.
An obstacle built into rules or institutions that blocks a whole group, not just one person.
The period after the Civil War (1865 to 1877) when the United States rebuilt and Black Americans gained, then lost, new rights.

9.scaffolding: Source-analysis template (who, when, purpose, testimony vs interpretation) with the first field modeled.

10.reading_level: Grade 10; comparative source analysis and structured argument.
10.essential_question: How did young people challenge the structures behind exclusion, not only single acts of it?

vocabulary.definition: Original evidence created by a participant or witness, used as direct material for historical analysis.
A systemic obstacle embedded in laws, institutions, or practices that restricts an entire group's access or opportunity.
The study of how history is written and how the perspective of the writer shapes the account.

10.scaffolding: Comparison matrix (individual act of exclusion vs structural barrier) across two clips; learners populate it before drafting.

11.reading_level: Grade 11; document-based analysis and evidence-based argumentation.
11.essential_question: How did representation, in textbooks and in who is allowed to lead, shape what young people had to confront?

vocabulary.definition: First-order evidence from a participant or witness, evaluated for perspective, purpose, and reliability.
An institutionalized constraint, encoded in policy or practice, that systematically disadvantages a defined group.
The analysis of how historical narratives are constructed, including the sources, framing, and biases of those who produce them.

11.scaffolding: Document-based-question scaffold: thesis frame, three evidence slots requiring entry-number-plus-timestamp citations, and a source-limitation note.

12.reading_level: Grade 12; independent source-criticism and seminar-level argument.
12.essential_question: How do oral-history testimony and the critique of how history is told reshape the story of who moved the Civil Rights Movement?

vocabulary.definition: Direct evidence from a participant or witness whose value depends on critical evaluation of provenance, perspective, and silences.
A systemic mechanism within institutions and law that reproduces unequal access independent of any single individual's intent.
The critical study of historical writing itself, examining how evidence, ideology, and the historian's standpoint determine the narrative that survives.

12.scaffolding: Seminar-and-essay protocol: learners self-select clips, construct a thesis, defend it against a counter-reading, and account for the limits of oral testimony as evidence.
